facts.title: Repair and reuse co-ops — Why it mattered
facts.discord_post:
🖤 Black Economics Fact #356: **Repair and reuse co-ops**
Repair and reuse co-ops shows that Black economics has often begun with circular economy: repair co-ops keep money local, teach skills, and reduce dependence on new purchases. The lesson is simple: Saving money together is also earning power.

💡 Try it today: Saving money together is also earning power.

facts.title: Cooperative procurement for businesses — Why it mattered
facts.discord_post:
🖤 Black Economics Fact #361: **Cooperative procurement for businesses**
Cooperative procurement for businesses shows that Black economics has often begun with B2B group buying: small businesses can jointly buy supplies, software, insurance, and shipping to negotiate better rates. The lesson is simple: Cooperation among businesses can lower costs without merging identities.

💡 Try it today: Cooperation among businesses can lower costs without merging identities.

S01.citation: Nembhard, J. G. (2014/2024). Collective Courage: A History of African American Cooperative Economic Thought and Practice. Pennsylvania State University Press / updated editions. https://books.google.com/books?id=GFIxEQAAQBAJ
S01.note: Foundational history of African American cooperative thought and practice.

S02.citation: Bartlett, J. T. (2016). Uncovering the hidden history of African-American cooperatives. Journal of Cultural Economy, 9(4), 341–357. https://doi.org/10.1080/17530350.2015.1135474
S02.note: Shows cooperative practices across Black communities and why they are often hidden in mainstream economic history.

S03.citation: Gordon-Nembhard, J. (2023). Black political economy, solidarity economics, and liberation: Toward an economy of caring and abundance. Review of Radical Political Economics. https://doi.org/10.1177/04866134231163216
S03.note: Connects Black political economy to mutual aid, solidarity economics, and liberation.

S04.citation: Hossein, C. S. (2017). The Black Social Economy in the Americas: Exploring Diverse Community-Based Markets. Palgrave Macmillan. https://books.google.com/books?id=Dnk2DwAAQBAJ
S04.note: Frames Black community finance and mutual aid as a social economy across the Americas.

S05.citation: Hossein, C. S. (2016). Money pools in the Americas: The African diaspora’s legacy in the social economy. Forum for Social Economics, 45(4), 309–328. https://doi.org/10.1080/07360932.2015.1114005
S05.note: Details susu, partner, box-hand, sol, tontine, and other diaspora money-pool systems.

S06.citation: Hossein, C. S., Austin, S. D. W., & Edmonds, K. (2023). Beyond Racial Capitalism: Co-operatives in the African Diaspora. Oxford University Press. https://books.google.com/books?id=XPGxEAAAQBAJ
S06.note: Surveys cooperative economics in the African diaspora.

S07.citation: Maynard, E. S. (1996). The translocation of a West African banking system: The Yoruba esusu rotating credit association in the Anglophone Caribbean. Dialectical Anthropology, 21, 113–134. https://www.jstor.org/stable/29790422
S07.note: Shows how Yoruba esusu practices travelled through the Caribbean.

S08.citation: Ardener, S. (1964). The comparative study of rotating credit associations. Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute, 94(2), 201–229. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2844382
S08.note: Classic comparative study of ROSCAs, including African and diaspora forms.

S09.citation: Josiah, B. P. (2004). Creating worlds: A study of mutuality and financing among African Guyanese, 1800s–1950s. Journal of Caribbean History, 38(1), 106–128.
S09.note: Explores African-Guyanese mutual finance, including box-hand/susu practices.

S10.citation: Stoffle, B. W., Stoffle, R. W., Minnis, J., & Van Vlack, K. (2014). Women’s power and community resilience: Rotating savings and credit associations in Barbados and the Bahamas. Caribbean Studies, 42(1), 3–37. https://muse.jhu.edu/article/565388
S10.note: Connects women-led ROSCAs to resilience and community capacity.

S11.citation: Patsides, N. (2007). Allies, constituents or myopic investors: Marcus Garvey and Black Americans. Journal of American Studies, 41(2), 279–305. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0021875807003479
S11.note: Analyzes Garveyism, investor relationships, and Black transnational organizing.

S12.citation: Harold, C. N. (2004). The Rise and Fall of the Garvey Movement in the Urban South, 1918–1942. PhD dissertation.
S12.note: Covers UNIA organizing in Southern cities and local economic networks.

S13.citation: Vinson, R. T. (2001). In the Time of the Americans: Garveyism in Segregationist South Africa, 1920–1940. PhD dissertation.
S13.note: Shows Garveyism’s influence in South Africa and transnational Black political economy.

S14.citation: Cummings, S. L. (2001). Community economic development as progressive politics: Toward a grassroots movement for economic justice. Stanford Law Review, 54(3), 399–493. https://www.jstor.org/stable/1229464
S14.note: Links community economic development to Black business, cooperative, and grassroots movements.

S15.citation: Price, D. A. (2017). Rich Black Soil: Co-operative Business Enterprise in the Jim Crow South, 1920–1938. PhD dissertation.
S15.note: Examines Black cooperative enterprise in the Jim Crow South.

S16.citation: Austin, S. D. W. (2023). The Black social economy. In Encyclopedia of the Social and Solidarity Economy. Edward Elgar. https://doi.org/10.4337/9781803920924.00020
S16.note: Defines Black social economy and diaspora mutual aid.

S17.citation: Walker, J. E. K. (2004). War, women, song: The tectonics of Black business and entrepreneurship, 1939–2001. Review of Black Political Economy, 31, 81–98. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12114-004-1005-3
S17.note: Discusses Black entrepreneurship, Black Power, and cooperatives.
